did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning methods, specifically Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN) for image enhancement and segmentation, and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) along with a modified ResNet-50 for classifying skin lesions. The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset to diagnose melanoma and other skin cancers, achieving an accuracy of 0.86.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a deep learning-based classification system for melanoma detection. It details specific methodologies including image enhancement using ESRGAN, segmentation, and classification using CNN and modified ResNet-50 models. The study utilizes the HAM10000 dataset, performs data augmentation, and reports original quantitative results (accuracy 0.86, precision 0.84, recall 0.86, F-score 0.86), distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Melanoma Detection Using Deep Learning-Based Classifications
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning-based system for automated melanoma detection and skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology employs Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN) for image enhancement and segmentation to isolate regions of interest, followed by data augmentation to address class imbalance. Classification is performed using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a modified ResNet-50 model. The proposed CNN-based model achieved an accuracy of 0.86, precision of 0.84, recall of 0.86, and an F-score of 0.86. These results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms earlier studies, offering an improved automated diagnostic tool for medical professionals.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic categories for pigmented lesions included in the HAM10000 dataset used for training and evaluation.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using a custom CNN and a modified ResNet-50. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs malignant or specific lesion types) rather than continuous values. Metrics reported include accuracy, precision, recall, F-score, and confusion matrices for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images collected from two locations: Vienna, Austria, and Queensland, Australia. The data was gathered using pre-digital cameras and stored in various formats (PowerPoint files, Excel databases). It is licensed under CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 and available via Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the proposed CNN model and fine-tuning the modified ResNet-50. The dataset was initially unbalanced (nv class 67%) and was subjected to data augmentation to ensure an even distribution of data before training.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the proposed CNN and modified ResNet-50 models. The study reports accuracy, precision, recall, and F-score on this dataset.
use.other: Used for segmentation to specify Regions of Interest (ROI) via ground truth images. Also used to enhance image visibility using ESRGAN.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To rectify data disparity (class imbalance) in the HAM10000 dataset by generating new samples and ensuring an even distribution of data across classes before training.

To achieve stacking generalization and maximize the functioning of DNN algorithms by running multiple training iterations of the same framework with randomly created weights, selecting the highest run outcome.

To evaluate the performance of the CNN and modified ResNet-50 models by displaying the number of correctly and incorrectly classified images per class (e.g., identifying nv lesions with 97% accuracy using CNN).

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F-score, Top N Accuracy
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively assess and compare the classification efficiency and effectiveness of the proposed deep learning models against the HAM10000 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F-score, and confusion matrices, with comparisons to other studies presented as descriptive performance metrics rather than statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.split: 90% training (9016 images), 10% testing (984 images), 10% of training set used for validation (992 images)
HAM10000.augmentation: Data augmentation increased training images to 39,430

Proposed CNN.notes: Outperformed earlier studies. Identified nv lesions with 97% accuracy (770 correctly classified out of 790). Average precision 0.84, recall 0.86, F-score 0.86 across 984 test images.

Modified ResNet-50.notes: Identified nv lesions with 94% accuracy (749 correctly classified out of 790). Average precision 0.86, recall 0.85, F-score 0.85 across 984 test images.

comparison_with_baselines.note: The proposed CNN (86%) and Modified ResNet-50 (85.3%) outperformed several baseline methods listed in Table 7.

architecture_used.main_model: Two models: a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a modified ResNet-50.
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. The custom CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x3 (for CNN preprocessing description) and 227x227x3 (for experimental configuration).

custom_cnn.structure: Four main layers plus an output layer. Each main layer consists of three convolution layers.
custom_cnn.convolution_layers: First two convolution layers have a kernel size of 3 and stride of 1. Final convolution layer has a kernel size of 5 and stride of 2.
custom_cnn.pooling: Three maxpooling layers with a pool size of 3 and stride of 1.

modified_resnet50.base: ResNet-50 (152-layer model based on residual units).
modified_resnet50.modifications: The initial fully connected (FC) layer was deleted and replaced with a new FC layer of size 512, another FC layer of size 3, and a new softmax layer. Two additional FC layers were added.
modified_resnet50.fine_tuning: Varying numbers of layers were frozen during fine-tuning.

hyperparameters.batch_size: Range from 2 to 64 (moving by two times the former value)
hyperparameters.learning_rate: Variable; ranged from 1x10^-4, 1x10^-5, and 1x10^-6 for CNN and ResNet-50 respectively. Used a technique that slows down learning when validation stagnates.

training_details.data_augmentation: Oversampling used to rectify class imbalance, increasing training images to 39,430.
training_details.ensemble_strategy: Many-runs ensemble (stacking generalization) with randomly created weights; highest run outcome selected.

classification_or_output_head.modified_resnet50: New FC layer of size 512, FC layer of size 3, and a new softmax layer.

preprocessing_techniques: Image enhancement using Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN) to improve image quality and eliminate noise.
Adaptive contrast enhancement technique applied to lesion images by rearranging brightness values in the histogram to improve border appearance and raise contrast.
Segmentation of Regions of Interest (ROI) using ground truth masks provided by the HAM10000 dataset.
Data augmentation to address class imbalance, including horizontal flipping, vertical flipping, rotation (0-180 degrees), and zooming.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set to address class imbalance and regularize data.
Applied to the training set to address class imbalance and regularize data.
Rotation angle between 0 and 180 degrees selected randomly.
Zoom range of 0.1 applied to images.
Adjusts image pixels horizontally while maintaining image dimension using an integer between zero and one indicating the step size.
Images resized to 224 × 224 × 3 (also mentioned as 244 × 244 × 3 in one instance) and rescaled by 1.0/255.
Used to rectify class imbalance, increasing the number of training images to 39,430.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning system, utilizing ESRGAN for image enhancement and segmentation followed by a custom CNN and modified ResNet-50 for classification, achieved an accuracy of 0.86, precision of 0.84, recall of 0.86, and F-score of 0.86 on the HAM10000 dataset.
The custom CNN-based model outperformed earlier studies and baseline methods (such as AlexNet, MobileNet, and standard ResNet-50) by a significant margin in classifying skin lesions.
The modified ResNet-50 model achieved an accuracy of 0.8526, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.85, and F-score of 0.85, demonstrating competitive performance compared to the custom CNN.
Data augmentation was effective in rectifying class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, ensuring an even distribution of data for training.
The 'many-runs ensemble' strategy was effective in maximizing the functioning of deep neural networks by selecting the highest outcome from multiple training iterations with randomly created weights.
The model identified melanocytic nevi (nv) lesions with high accuracy (97% for CNN and 94% for modified ResNet-50), which is critical for real-world applications given the prevalence of this class.
The study demonstrates that the proposed method offers an improved automated diagnostic tool for medical professionals and patients, facilitating early detection of skin cancer.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is geographically biased (collected from Vienna, Austria, and Queensland, Australia), limiting external validation and generalizability to other populations.

The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance prior to augmentation, with Melanocytic nevi (nv) comprising 67% of samples, while rare classes like Dermatofibroma (df) and Vascular lesions (vasc) have very few samples (115 and 149 respectively).

Performance on rare classes is notably weak; for example, the proposed CNN achieved only 0.32 F-score for Melanoma (mel) and 0.18 F-score for Dermatofibroma (df), indicating poor sensitivity for critical malignant and rare benign cases.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for the comparison between the proposed models and baselines, relying instead on descriptive metric comparisons.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of ground truth masks provided by the HAM10000 dataset for segmentation; if the segmentation step inadvertently uses information correlated with the label during training, it could inflate classification performance.

The study claims the proposed CNN outperforms earlier studies by a 'significant margin,' but the reported accuracy (0.86) is comparable to or lower than several cited baselines (e.g., RegNetY-3.2GF at 85.8%, MobileNet and LSTM at 85%), making the claim of superiority ambiguous without direct head-to-head comparison under identical conditions.

The model was trained on a balanced dataset created via aggressive data augmentation (oversampling), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence where benign lesions are far more common than malignant ones.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about its robustness in diverse clinical settings.

The paper acknowledges that lesion-less skin and confounding factors are not included in the current dataset, which limits the model's applicability in real-world screening where non-lesional skin is common.
